title: GDPR Access Control Review and Privileged Access Evidence

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.32(1)(b) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.32(1)(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.22: GDPR Access Control Review and Privileged Access Evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.22' running on CIM Authentication data model, OktaIM2:log, ms:aad:signin, cyberark:session, gdpr_system_access_matrix.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` latest(Authentication.user) as user latest(Authentication.app) as app
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  where Authentication.action="success" AND earliest=-30d
  by Authentication.dest
| rename Authentication.* as *
| lookup gdpr_system_access_matrix.csv system_hostname AS dest OUTPUT data_classification, approved_roles
| where data_classification IN ("personal_data","special_category")
| join type=left user [
    search index=okta sourcetype="OktaIM2:log" eventType="group.privilege.grant" earliest=-30d
    | stats latest(_time) as last_grant by target_user_id
    | rename target_user_id as user
]
| where isnotnull(last_grant)
| table dest, user, app, data_classification, approved_roles, last_grant
description: Article 32(1)(b) requires measures to ensure ongoing confidentiality, including controlling access to personal data. This use case reviews who can access GDPR-scoped systems — flagging excessive admin grants, dormant privileged accounts, and role changes that expand access beyond ROPA-documented purposes — giving auditors repeatable evidence of access governance.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes okta and sourcetypes OktaIM2:log. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map hosts and SaaS apps in `gdpr_system_access_matrix.csv` with approved IAM roles; (2) ingest IdP group and role assignment events; (3) correlate successful authentications to personal-data systems; (4) alert when users access GDPR systems without an approved role or after a recent privilege grant; (5) export monthly access review packets for data owners. Indexes required: `index=okta`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=OktaIM2:log`.
visualization: Table (privileged access drift), Bar chart (access by system), Single value (users outside approved roles), Heatmap (logon density to PII systems).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services

knownFalsePositives: DBA and after-hours activity during approved maintenance, large ETL jobs, disaster recovery drills, or work already covered by a change record.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CIM Authentication data model, `OktaIM2:log`, `ms:aad:signin`, `cyberark:session`, `gdpr_system_access_matrix.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Map hosts and SaaS apps in `gdpr_system_access_matrix.csv` with approved IAM roles; (2) ingest IdP group and role assignment events; (3) correlate successful authentications to personal-data systems; (4) alert when users access GDPR systems without an approved role or after a recent privilege grant; (5) export monthly access review packets for data owners.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` latest(Authentication.user) as user latest(Authentication.app) as app
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  where Authentication.action="success" AND earliest=-30d
  by Authentication.dest
| rename Authentication.* as *
| lookup gdpr_system_access_matrix.csv system_hostname AS dest OUTPUT data_classification, approved_roles
| where data_classification IN ("personal_data","special_category")
| join type=left user [
    search index=okta sourcetype="OktaIM2:log" eventType="group.privilege.grant" earliest=-30d
    | stats latest(_time) as last_grant by target_user_id
    | rename target_user_id as user
]
| where isnotnull(last_grant)
| table dest, user, app, data_classification, approved_roles, last_grant
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Access Control Review and Privileged Access Evidence (Art. 32(1)(b))** — Article 32(1)(b) requires measures to ensure ongoing confidentiality, including controlling access to personal data. This use case reviews who can access GDPR-scoped systems — flagging excessive admin grants, dormant privileged accounts, and role changes that expand access beyond ROPA-documented purposes — giving auditors repeatable evidence of access governance.

Documented **Data sources**: CIM Authentication data model, `OktaIM2:log`, `ms:aad:signin`, `cyberark:session`, `gdpr_system_access_matrix.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where data_classification IN ("personal_data","special_category")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(last_grant)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: app, approved_roles, data_classification, dest, last_grant, user.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (privileged access drift), Bar chart (access by system), Single value (users outside approved roles), Heatmap (logon density to PII systems).
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of access control review and privileged access evidence — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
